title: Esther 6
subtitle: The King’s Sleepless Night; Haman’s Humiliation

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Esther Saves Her People

heb.paragraph: Sleep "fled" the king. Not "could not sleep" but "sleep fled." In a book of hidden agency, even insomnia has a subject — who caused sleep to flee?

hist.historical: Reading royal chronicles as a sleep remedy was Persian court practice. That the king chose this night to read this section is the book’s master coincidence.
hist.context: This is the pivot. The king’s insomnia → chronicles read → Mordecai’s unrewarded service discovered → Haman arrives to request execution → collision. The timing is impossible as coincidence and perfect as providence.

refs.note: Pharaoh’s dream — divine intervention through a king’s disturbed night.

notes.note: That night the king could not sleep. Seven words that change everything. No angel, no vision. Just insomnia.
The chronicles reveal Mordecai’s unrewarded service from chapter 2. Years-old information, now activated.
Nothing has been done for him. The king is appalled.
Haman enters to request Mordecai’s execution at the exact moment the king wants to honour Mordecai. The collision is the most dramatic irony in the Hebrew Bible.
Calvin: God did not need an earthquake. He simply took sleep. The smallest instrument accomplishes the greatest purpose.
Calvin: how perfectly God arranges the collision of human intentions.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes the passive: literally "the sleep of the king was disturbed." The agent is unstated.
The NET observes chronicles were read aloud by court scribes.
Levenson: sleep fled. The verb implies active departure. Something drove sleep away.
Levenson: as Pharaoh’s dream elevated Joseph, the king’s insomnia elevates Mordecai.
Jobes: the king’s insomnia is the most theologically loaded event in the book. The narrator does not say God caused it. The reader draws the conclusion.
Jobes: neither knows what the other intends. The reader knows both. Purest dramatic irony in Scripture.

sections.header: Verses 6–11 — "What Should Be Done for the Man the King Delights to Honour?"

heb.paragraph: Haman assumes the honour is for himself. His pride designs his own humiliation. Every detail he specifies, he must perform for Mordecai.

refs.note: Whoever digs a pit will fall into it.
The Lord laughs at the wicked.

notes.note: Whom would the king delight to honour more than me? Pride is the mechanism of his own humiliation.
Royal robe, royal horse, crown, public herald. Maximum visibility — for the wrong recipient.
Do just as you have suggested for Mordecai the Jew. Do not neglect anything. Every detail.
Haman leads Mordecai through the streets shouting his own defeat.
Calvin: pride blinded Haman so completely that he could not imagine the honour was for anyone else.
Calvin: the reversal is so complete that only God’s providence accounts for it.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes the royal robe and horse suggest investiture — the honouree as the king’s representative.
The king says "Mordecai the Jew" — linking the honour to the condemned people.
Levenson: the reversal is perfect. The Hebrew nahapōk (reversal) is the structural keyword.
Jobes: the comic centrepiece. Haman scripts his own humiliation in exquisite detail. Comedy and justice merge.

hist.context: Pure comedy and pure theology. Haman designs an elaborate honour ceremony for himself, then executes it for his enemy. His imagination creates his own punishment.

sections.header: Verses 12–14 — Haman Rushes Home; Zeresh Predicts His Fall

heb.paragraph: The doubled verb expresses certainty. Even Haman’s wife sees the pattern: opposing Jews leads to destruction.

refs.note: Whoever curses you I will curse — the promise Zeresh unknowingly invokes.

notes.note: Mordecai returns to his post unmoved. Haman rushes home in grief. The contrast is total.
If Mordecai is of Jewish origin, you cannot stand against him. His own advisors invoke covenant theology from outside the covenant.
While still talking, eunuchs arrive to hurry Haman to the banquet. No time to process. The pace accelerates.
Calvin: even pagans recognise that God protects his people.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes "of Jewish origin" uses zeraʿ (seed), the covenant term.
Levenson: the reversal of 5:10–14 is mathematically precise. The structure argues for governing intelligence.
Jobes: Zeresh’s words are the closest thing to a theological statement in the book. She invokes covenant theology from outside the covenant.

hist.context: Reverses 5:10–14 exactly. There: boasting, gallows advised. Here: mourning, doom predicted. Same cast, opposite advice.

ppl.name: Haman
ppl.role: The man who honours his enemy
ppl.text: Every detail of the ceremony he imagined for himself, he performs for Mordecai.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThen let the robe and horse be entrusted to one of the king’s most noble princes. Let them robe the man the king delights to honor, and lead him on the horse through the city streets, proclaiming before him, ‘This is what is done for the man the king delights to honor!’ ”ESVAnd let the robes and the horse be handed over to one of the king’s most noble officials. Let them dress the man whom the king delights to honor, and let them lead him on the horse through the square of the city, proclaiming before him: ‘Thus shall it be done to the man whom the king delights to honor.’”NIV“What honor and recognition has Mordecai received for this?” the king asked. “Nothing has been done for him,” his attendants answered.ESVAnd the king said, “What honor or distinction has been bestowed on Mordecai for this?” The king’s young men who attended him said, “Nothing has been done for him.”

rec.text: "That night the king could not sleep. Seven words that change everything. No angel, no vision. Just insomnia."

rows.label: The Prediction — 6:12–14
rows.text: You will surely fall. Zeresh sees what Haman cannot.

hebtext.note: Sleep "fled" the king. Not "could not sleep" but "sleep fled." In a book of hidden agency, even insomnia has a subject — who caused sleep to flee?
Haman assumes the honour is for himself. His pride designs his own humiliation. Every detail he specifies, he must perform for Mordecai.
The doubled verb expresses certainty. Even Haman’s wife sees the pattern: opposing Jews leads to destruction.

themes.note: Sovereignty at 10: the sleepless night is providence at its most invisible and decisive.
